Executive Summary
About Borouge: Borouge is a leading petrochemical company that provides creative plastics solutions for
a wide range of industries. Established in 1998, Borouge is a joint venture between the Abu Dhabi National
Oil Company (ADNOC), one of the world’s leading oil and gas companies and Austria based Borealis.
petrochemical and plastics manufacturing plant is in Al Ruwais at about 250 km west of Abu Dhabi. In
addition to our production site in Abu Dhabi, Borouge also has a Compounding Manufacturing Plant (CMP)
in Shanghai which compounds resins from the Ruwais plant specifically for the Asian automotive industry.
Supply Chain Design & Planning Model:
Need
As a world leader in polymer solutions, we required an established digital twin for its supply chain
planning (more specifically logistic network optimization & simulation) and configurable assets. The initial
transformation process focused on UAE production site, global supply chain network and operations, with
an emphasis on cost and service levers. The need was to develop a digital twin within the first 8 months
of the project. The solution was to be used as a strategic tool to define the optimal network. This was to
be done by selecting the optimal Inventory Holding Point ("IHP") to serve customers based on associated
costs, capacities and lead times.
Key Objectives
The primary objective of the Supply Chain Network Optimization & Simulation model was to optimize the
Inventory Holding Point ("IHP") selection between Al Ruwais and the destination port to minimize network
cost leading to better
a.
Visibility
b.
Capability
c.
Autonomy
d.
Cost Savings
Development Brief
The model was developed using:

a.
A baseline based on 12 months production plan, demand forecast, historic transport, network
inventory position, planned site closures
b.
An optimized network without following predefined flows in production plan
c.
Transportation Modeling
i. Packaging facility to UAE IHPs – road, sea freight and rail modeled
ii. UAE IHPs to International IHPs – sea freight modeled
iii.All IHPs to Destination Port – sea freight modeled
iv. Destination Port to Customer – not modeled
d.
Costs: Packing, Handling, Transport ($/ton), Storage ($/ton/mo)
e.
Constraints: Packing capacity (tons/mo per packing line), transport lane capacity (tons/mo), inventory
capacity (tons/mo at all IHPs) & transit times / service levels
f.
Scenarios where existing constraints can be removed, modified and new constraints could be
introduced
g.
Frequency: Monthly cadence runs utilizing M0-M11 (12 months production plan and demand)
h.
Processes included ingestion of data, cleansing and transformation of data, model building, scenarios
building and transforming model output to user consumable reports.
Outcome
A Network Optimization Model utilizing applications like Supply Chain Guru X & Data Guru was created
that lead to following outcomes and certain capabilities for the end user:
a.
The model outputs enabled comparison of different scenarios based on criteria such as LVC (Logistics
Variable Cost), DIT (Delay in Transit) utilization, & ending inventory
b.
The model provided volumes, at a material level, to be shipped from Ruwais to IHPs and from IHPs
to destination ports
c.
The information was then subsequently shared to the relevant teams for execution
d.
Adjustments to the model take the form of what is known as scenarios. Example scenarios/model
recommendations included the following:
i. Optimize IHP Selection - Allow all/specific customers to have IHP optimized
ii. Modify SLA (Service Level Agreements) group ratios
iii.Modify site throughput/storage capacity
iv. Modify site packaging capacity/capability
v. Close IHP/Open IHP
vi. Alter transport costs
vii. Modify port sourcing ratios by customer
viii.  Define volume ratio split between DIT and Warehouse IHPs at customer product level
ix. Constraints applied at the calendar year level – E.x. minimum yearly site throughput
x. Feeder leg ratio adjustments – The feeder leg is the lane between plant and UAE IHPs; used to
adjust ratio of volume to be split between rail/sea
xi. Ability to group destination ports and apply flow count constraints to limit the count of IHPs
servicing all the ports in a group
Results
a.
Model identified potential cost savings of up to 9% of supply chain logistics cost compared to the
baseline for the 12-month period. The model outputs enable comparison of different scenarios based
on criteria such as LVC, DIT utilization, ending inventory.

8
SPE-222960-MS
Creating the baseline model.   The model was first used to develop the baseline which would reflect current
plans for the next 12 months. This will allow the model to generate a base for comparison when running
multiple scenarios. It is important to first develop an optimal network scenario which disables replicating
product flows as per the baseline to provide a view of what could be achieved before implementing
constraints in other scenarios. The optimal network scenario along with the different scenarios already
developed will be explained in the scenarios section within this paper.
Model echelons.   The baseline model consists of four echelons, i.e. the plant, IHPs, followed by the ports
(InPo) and then the end customers. Historical movement of all products through all the lanes traversed in
historical transport data, beginning from the plant and ending at the destination port have been duplicated
to form a baseline model. There are four echelons of transport in the global supply chain network, and they
are the plant to UAE IHPs (road, sea freight and rail), followed by UAE IHPs to international IHPs (sea
freight), followed by all IHPs to destination ports (sea freight) and finally from the destination ports (InPo)
to customer. However, note that costs in the last leg are not modeled in the network.
Model costs.   The various costs that have been modeled are the packing costs (only available at the Al
Ruwais plant and two other IHPs), handling costs, storage costs and transport costs. The former three
costs together form part of fixed and variable costs associated with the sites in the models (plant, IHP and
InPo) and the latter cost is associated with the transport legs within the model. Thus, all the costs used in
transportation, production and associated costs with the IHP and InPos have been modelled and when added
up form the total cost of the baseline scenario, Model constraints
Constraints have been applied to packing (production constraints table), transport lane capacity (flow
and flow count constraints table), inventory capacity (inventory constraints table) and transit times
(Transportation policies table). Also, the live inventory position is also loaded into the baseline and is the
beginning inventory for PERIOD_00 which is the first period within the model (PERIOD_11 being the last).
Miscellaneous configurations.   Note that a dummy plant beside the Al Ruwais facility, named as
MFG_0000 has also been configured into the model so that demand which cannot be satisfied due to specific
constraints is then satisfied by the dummy plant. The volumes that are satisfied by the dummy plant needs
to be minimal, to conclude that the baseline is satisfactorily working.
Scenarios.   The final stage in developing the model is creating scenarios which are broken down into two
sections, Scenarios and Scenario Items. Scenarios consist of a collection of scenario items. A scenario item
identifies the action that will be taken to adjust the model outcome, either constraining the model or relaxing
it, to allow the model to act in a way that the modeler feels suits the needs of their operations. As part of
the network optimization project, several scenario items and scenarios were developed.
Scenario example (Optimize IHP selection).   Note that a scenario item consists of changes made to a
single table within the model. Multiple scenario items are thus needed to implement a single scenario. As an
example, consider the second scenario in the table below. It’s purpose is to optimize the baseline scenario
by removing restrictions on the network. Currently when creating the baseline, the modeler needs to make
sure that all flows that are keyed in, need to mimic the actual flows that have happened so as to arrive
at a total cost that is reasonably close to the actual cost incurred for the supply chain network. But these
flows may not be intuitive when it comes to saving costs, and hence any constraints that have been applied
(which are generally implemented on multiple tables within SCGX), need to be disabled. Coming back to
our example, all flows which mimic the baseline are stored within the flow constraints table and hence a
scenario item is created for this purpose. Next, there are flow count constraints which set limits on how
many sites a customer can source from or how many plants a site can source from, etc. These can also be
relaxed so that the model can arrive at a solution that is not limited to the flow counts imposed on it to mimic
the baseline flows. To summarize, the ‘Optimize IHP selection’ scenario consists of two scenario items, one
which disables baseline flows and the other disables baseline flow count constraints.

Combining three scenarios into one
Once these scenarios were created, the baseline model was run, along with these scenarios and the total
cost for each scenario is displayed below.
Outputs and visualizations
Standard model outputs.   The outputs created by SCGX are in the form of tables which can be exported
as Excel files. The output tables broadly have two categories – summary and flow (transactional) tables.
Summary tables take one aspect of the supply chain and summarize required transactions and attributes by
that aspect. Examples are network summary (summary of total profit, costs and revenue), network customer
summary, network site summary, network product summary, network inventory summary, customer demand
summary and constraint summary. As an example, the network site summary summarizes all transactions
(product flow, costs, open/close status, etc.) by all the sites within the network, whereas the customer demand
summary summarizes all transactions (total demand, demand served, costs, etc.) and customer attributes by
each individual customer within the network. Transactional tables contain each transaction at a row level
and examples are customer flows, inter-site flows and production flows. As an example, customer flows
contain data grouped at customer, site, period and product level, with each row signifying the product moved
within a specific period from a specific site to a specific customer whereas inter-site flows are similar but
contain product flows from site to site.

Modified outputs using DG.   However, these standard output tables may require some level of data
transformations to convert them into standardized reports fit for internal use and hence, DG has been used
again to convert these standard tables into specifically formatted Excel reports. Examples are Lost Demand
Report, LVC report, Network Cost Report, Transport Cost Report RWS to IHP and Transport Cost Report
IHP to InPo. The Lost Demand report simply details any demand that is not satisfied. The LVC report breaks
down all costs with each flow at an origin, material, destination and period level. The network cost report
summarizes costs by each scenario and finally the two transport cost reports detail volumes and costs for
lanes between the Al Ruwais plant and the IHPs and IHPs to InPos respectively.
Output visualizations.   Next, output visualizations have been created in a QlikView environment which
includes standard visualizations reporting on various aspects of the supply chain network such as costs,
volume, etc. as well as maps showing the various flows within the supply chain.
Challenges encountered and approach used.   A critical element in supply chain design projects is the
need to validate intermediate steps before proceeding to subsequent ones. An example of this is the need to
validate and approve the data set that will be used in the model after the initial data has been aggregated,
manipulated, harmonized, and completed. Another example is the need to validate the baseline before
proceeding to scenario building. It often happens that those responsible for approving these intermediate
steps have difficulty doing so if they do not have a perfect understanding of the nature of the project, the
technology, and the assumptions that have been made.
To address this issue, the approach followed was to accompany the project’s development with continuous
training and the ongoing transfer of skills and knowledge. This approach allowed decision-makers to
approve intermediate steps by providing them with all the necessary information and knowledge required
for an informed and straightforward evaluation and validation of both intermediate and final steps.
Another critical point in the development of these projects is that very often the scope or assumptions
defined initially need to be revised during the project due to the complex and dynamic nature of the
supply chain design process. To adhere to the initially defined timelines and budget, a continuous, attentive,
and flexible project management methodology was adopted. This methodology focuses on budget and
time control while respecting the project’s overall objectives. A robust, repeatable planning process was
implemented along with knowledge transfer to enable continuous and constant monitoring of activity
progress. This allows for adjustments when necessary, ensuring maximum awareness and speed from the
team responsible for making these decisions.
Another important aspect to consider in these types of projects is the need to involve all levels of the
organization from the outset to fully understand the process and development methodology. There is a risk
in such projects that as activities progress, the value may not seem immediately apparent. In fact, value
is identified only at the end of the project during the optimization phase related to the development of
alternative scenarios. To reduce the risk associated with the intrinsic nature of this methodology, it is crucial
to involve all stakeholders so that the initiative has the organization’s support, which understands its nature,
timelines, and methodology.
It is also important to inform all involved parties throughout the project phases about the nature of the
technology used. This technology differs from classic reporting or cost calculation systems due to its ability
to add intelligence to basic information to develop optimization scenarios that can support both strategic
and tactical decisions. From this perspective, it is essential to understand the need to shift from the level
of detail typical of transactional systems that analyze data at the maximum level of detail to a model that
operates at a higher level of approximation using aggregation and simplification criteria.
Another important aspect to consider is that in some phases of the project, it is possible to parallelize
certain activities. For example, a thorough evaluation of the baseline for its validation at the highest level
of detail can be paralleled with the beginning of optimization scenario construction. This approach allows

maintaining a project plan within the initially defined horizon while extending the expected duration of
some specific activities.
Another challenge was understanding complex operational level challenges like production lines,
packaging capacities and converting them to strategic level requirements in the network. Next, optimizing
the ports to be considered in the network out of thousands of ports used from historical data to few hundreds
to reduce the complexity was an important task. Also, we needed additional support from their planners
to correct the product plan to match the future demand and remove correct the imbalances. There were
multiple scenario requirements from different business users, and so the team had to come up with minimal
building blocks required which can in combination help them to create most of their scenarios.
Conclusions
Implementing global supply chain network as a digital twin has unlocked multiple benefits such as having a
cost associated with each echelon of the network, having access to multiple reports and views of the current
as-is network as well as ‘simulated’ networks, having a method to quantitatively assess the advantages and
disadvantages of choosing a different network strategy over an existing network, and an ability to detect
in advance any issues that may arise in the course of the 12 month period of the network model besides
other advantages.
Due to the nature of the implemented model, the stakeholder can drill down into the costs and other
attributes of the model to provide the inner workings of the network in the form of reports and visualizations
in a much shorter time than before. Without the digital model, the stakeholder would have to transform
raw data from scratch into the specific report required. Multiple what-if scenarios can be run and compared
alongside the baseline scenario in terms of costs, service level and other attributes if so required. This gives
a quantitative method to evaluate and go ahead with a different strategy as opposed to the current one. Also,
as the model is a multi-period model, the reports coming out of the model will give a 12-month future view
of the flows and operations within the network and can be used to alert stakeholders should there be any
bottlenecks that appear in periods in the future such as shortage of inventory at a specific note, unavailability
of material to satisfy demand, etc.
As also mentioned within the results sub-section and the image below, the optimized baseline models
(scenarios 5, 10, 11 and 12) show a cost savings of around 9%.
